statusLine: EU fuel security is under strain with 13 of 15 reporting countries holding diesel reserves below safe thresholds, and Brent crude at $111.21 amid Middle East supply disruptions.
fullAnalysis:
European fuel security faces significant pressure as the majority of EU countries struggle to maintain adequate emergency reserves. While EU-wide averages show petrol at 94.5 days and diesel at 95.8 days—marginally above the 90-day mandatory minimum—this masks severe disparities across member states. Romania presents the most strained situation with diesel reserves at just 20.2 days, followed by Poland at 71 days and Austria at 74.8 days. Thirteen of the 15 countries currently reporting hold diesel reserves at levels below safe thresholds. Not all 27 EU member states have yet reported for this period; Eurostat data carries an approximate 2-month lag.

Jet fuel reserves present an even more concerning picture at 89.9 days EU-wide average, falling below the mandatory minimum. Poland's jet fuel reserves are particularly low at 21.3 days, with Portugal at 23.1 days. Only Belgium maintains adequate jet fuel stocks at 97.6 days. The aviation fuel shortfall could significantly impact European air transport and connectivity.

Oil prices continue climbing with Brent crude reaching $111.21 per barrel, up 1.31% and approximately 27% since the current Middle East conflict began. This price pressure, combined with depleted strategic reserves due to recent releases during supply disruptions, leaves the EU in a vulnerable position. Consumer fuel prices reflect this strain, with petrol averaging €1.789 per litre and diesel at €2.00 per litre, though significant variation exists between countries like Malta (€1.34) and Netherlands (€2.334) for petrol.
keyPoints: 13 of 15 reporting countries hold diesel reserves below safe thresholds — Romania at 20.2 days is the most exposed (not all 27 EU members have reported yet)
EU jet fuel reserves at 89.9 days fall below the 90-day mandatory minimum for emergency stocks
Brent crude has surged 27% since the Middle East conflict began, now at $111.21 per barrel
Eastern European countries show the most severe shortfalls, with Poland and Romania under pressure across all fuel types
Strategic reserve releases during recent supply disruptions have left the EU with a reduced buffer for future shocks
